titlestmt.titleproper: Stroud, John, collection on Middle East aviation
Guide to the John H. Stroud collection on Middle East aviation AD.MC.087
titlestmt.author: Brad Bauer

publicationstmt.publisher: NYU Abu Dhabi, Archives and Special Collections

address.addressline: New York University Abu Dhabi, C-2

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2026-04-30 22:06:50 +0400.
profiledesc.langusage: Description is written in: English, Latin script.

repository.corpname: NYU Abu Dhabi, Archives and Special Collections

did.unittitle: John H. Stroud collection on Middle East aviation
did.abstract: Photographs, maps, correspondence, ephemera, and documents, collected by British aviation photographer, artist, and journalist John H. Stroud, related to commercial airlines in the Middle East chiefly during the 1950s and 1960s.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions governing access
accessrestrict.p: John H. Stroud Collection on Middle East Aviation is available for use in the Archives and Special Collections department of the New York University Abu Dhabi Library.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The collection is arranged in one series, largely in alphabetical order by the names of airlines.

bioghist.head: Biographical note
bioghist.p: John Hector Stroud (1919-2007) was born on 3 April 1919 in Balham, London, the son of Alfred and Laura (née Archer) Stroud.

Later in life, Stroud recalled seeing his first aircraft at the airport in Croydon when he was four years old, but that his interest in aviation grew once his family moved to Hatfield, Hertfordshire, in 1929, in particular once he began seeing some of the Imperial Airways H.P. 42 and 45 aircraft during their test flights at the Handley Page factory in nearby Radlett.

When he was in his teens, he worked for Imperial Airways, designing their blue and silver livery used on their DH.91 Albatross fleet. His work as an illustrator was featured in several articles books during World War II, focusing on Chinese, Russian, and Japanese military aircraft. He also created an exhibition for the general public on aircraft identification, particularly relevant to Britons who were subject to frequent German bombing raids during the war.

Following World War II, his interests shifted to civil aviation. In 1954 he became a regular columnist on the topic of aviation in The Journal of Commerce, a post that he held until 1985. In 1961 he became general editor for Putnam's Aviation List (later Putnam Aviation Books and Putnam Aeronautical Books), overseeing the publication of numerous reference books on individual aircraft manufacturers, the history of British aviation, and airports.
John H. Stroud died on 14 March 2007.
Source: "John Stroud: illustrator, aviation expert and historian," on the website http://www.hatfield-herts.co.uk/aviation/stroud.html, accessed on 30 April 2026.

prefercite.head: Preferred citation
prefercite.p: [item], [folder title], John H. Stroud Collection on Middle East Aviation, MC-087, New York University Abu Dhabi Library, Abu Dhabi, UAE.

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: John H. Stroud Collection on Middle East Aviation contains photographs, maps, correspondence, ephemera, and documents, collected by British aviation photographer, artist, and journalist John H. Stroud, related to commercial airlines in the Middle East chiefly during the 1950s and 1960s.

The items in the collection appear to have been collected by Stroud in the course of his work as a journalist and artist, researching both the aircraft and companies that he depicted and wrote about in aviation trade magazines. In some cases, he had contacts within specific aviation companies, in particular the Beirut-based Middle East Airlines and the Cairo-based Misr Air (forerunner of today's Egyptair), as his correspondence documents his requests for information, and in some cases, visits to the region--in particular Lebanon--to observe the airlines operations firsthand.

The collection contains photographs of aircraft used by various companies, including Middle East Airlines, Misr Air, Kuwait Airways, Iraqi Airways, and Gulf Aviation, with the images usually produced by the British manufacturers of the aircraft that they used during this period, chiefly from Hawker Siddeley, but also Vickers, de Havilland and other companies. In addition to photographs and correspondence, there is a small amount of handwritten and typed notes, as well as route maps, timetables, advertisements, and a published article from Stroud, which provides a sample of the type of research and writing he was engaged in.

The present material represents but a fraction of Stroud's actual archive, which according to online reports, was split up and auctioned following Stroud's death in 2007, and which included materials related to civil aviation in a wide range of countries outside of the Middle East.

userestrict.head: Conditions governing use
userestrict.p: Unpublished as well as published content within the collection may be protected by copyright, and any uses of such material beyond what is commonly accepted as "fair use" require the express written permission of the copyright holder.

did.unittitle: Iraqi Airways : promotional brochure and timetable
Iraqi Airways : photographic prints (5), depicting aircraft
Kuwait Airways : photographic prints (8) of aircraft
Middle East Airlines : correspondence between company representatives and Stroud
Middle East Airlines : news clipping of article by Stroud, advertisement, letterhead, load sheet, route map
Middle East Airlines : photographs (2) of aircraft
Misr Air / United Arab Airlines : correspondence
Misr Air / United Arab Airlines : miscellaneous, including advertisements, clippings, notes
Misr Air / United Arab Airlines : photographs (2), one of aircraft, one of man standing in front of office
Maps (4) : rainfall charts for Sahara Desert, prohibited flight zones in northern Egypt
Photographs (2) : one of Gulf Aviation aircraft, two of de Havilland Puss Moth planes flying over Palestine
